**Session 2022-23**

Section heading: Our use of cookies

**Session 2022-23**

chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written** **e vidence s ubmitted by the Internet Advertising Bureau UK (IAB UK) (OSB33)**

The Internet Advertising Bureau UK (IAB UK) welcomes the opportunity to contribute to this consultation. The Online Safety Bill is a world-leading piece of legislation which will have a major impact on the British people’s experience of the internet.

IAB UK i s the trade association for digital advertising, bring ing together 1,200 members including media owners, ad vertising tech nology intermediaries, agencies, and brands.Our purpose is to build a sustainable future for digital advertising, a market that was worth £ 23.47bn in the UK in 202 1.

**Executive summary**

This submission will focus on the standalone duty relating to advertising fraud in the Online Safety Bill.

We were disappointed that industry was not consulted on the standalone duty and concerned that it was inserted into the Bill so late into the process of legislative development, seemingly in response to external pressures rather than considered policy development. More importantly, the duty pre-empts the Online Advertising Programme (OAP), which the Government itself has said is the appropriate vehicle for considering any changes to the regulatory framework for our industry, and which includes paid-for fraudulent advertising in scope. The OAP is being developed via a consultative process that looks at the complex online advertising ecosystem in the round, meaning it is the right approach for properly identifying where potential issues may exist that require regulatory solutions, and what the most appropriate solutions are.
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written** **e vidence s ubmitted by the Internet Advertising Bureau UK (IAB UK) (OSB33)**

The Internet Advertising Bureau UK (IAB UK) welcomes the opportunity to contribute to this consultation. The Online Safety Bill is a world-leading piece of legislation which will have a major impact on the British people’s experience of the internet.

IAB UK i s the trade association for digital advertising, bring ing together 1,200 members including media owners, ad vertising tech nology intermediaries, agencies, and brands.Our purpose is to build a sustainable future for digital advertising, a market that was worth £ 23.47bn in the UK in 202 1.

**Executive summary**

This submission will focus on the standalone duty relating to advertising fraud in the Online Safety Bill.

We were disappointed that industry was not consulted on the standalone duty and concerned that it was inserted into the Bill so late into the process of legislative development, seemingly in response to external pressures rather than considered policy development. More importantly, the duty pre-empts the Online Advertising Programme (OAP), which the Government itself has said is the appropriate vehicle for considering any changes to the regulatory framework for our industry, and which includes paid-for fraudulent advertising in scope. The OAP is being developed via a consultative process that looks at the complex online advertising ecosystem in the round, meaning it is the right approach for properly identifying where potential issues may exist that require regulatory solutions, and what the most appropriate solutions are.

chunks.text: paid-for fraudulent advertising in scope. The OAP is being developed via a consultative process that looks at the complex online advertising ecosystem in the round, meaning it is the right approach for properly identifying where potential issues may exist that require regulatory solutions, and what the most appropriate solutions are. We therefore strongly urge the Committee to consider recommending that the standalone duty on online advertising is removed from the Online Safety Bill to enable a genuinely holistic review of digital advertising regulation to take place through the OAP. **The fraudulent advertising duty** At introduction, our membership was surprised to see that the scope of the Online Safety Bill had been expanded to include a new standalone duty relating to fraudulent advertising. This duty will require Category 1 and 2A services (i.e. the biggest social media and search companies) to take action to minimise the likelihood of fraudulent adverts being published on their platforms. IAB UK and our members agree that fraudulent online advertisements can cause serious harm. Our declaration on Scam Ads (attached at annex A) outlines our members’ ongoing commitment to address the issue of financial scam advertising. We believe that industry, Government, regulators, and law enforcement need to work together to make the UK a hostile environment for this criminal activity. Effective law enforcement is needed to address the criminal activity behind these scams, to complement industry efforts to identify and disrupt those seeking to disseminate scam ads. IAB understands the logic of including user-to-user fraud ulent content in scope of the Online Safety Bill, given that the Bill is designed to regulate firms which host user-generated content. In principle, this intervention is aligned with the Bill’s purpose.
chunks.display_text: We therefore strongly urge the Committee to consider recommending that the standalone duty on online advertising is removed from the Online Safety Bill to enable a genuinely holistic review of digital advertising regulation to take place through the OAP. **The fraudulent advertising duty** At introduction, our membership was surprised to see that the scope of the Online Safety Bill had been expanded to include a new standalone duty relating to fraudulent advertising. This duty will require Category 1 and 2A services (i.e. the biggest social media and search companies) to take action to minimise the likelihood of fraudulent adverts being published on their platforms. IAB UK and our members agree that fraudulent online advertisements can cause serious harm. Our declaration on Scam Ads (attached at annex A) outlines our members’ ongoing commitment to address the issue of financial scam advertising. We believe that industry, Government, regulators, and law enforcement need to work together to make the UK a hostile environment for this criminal activity. Effective law enforcement is needed to address the criminal activity behind these scams, to complement industry efforts to identify and disrupt those seeking to disseminate scam ads. IAB understands the logic of including user-to-user fraud ulent content in scope of the Online Safety Bill, given that the Bill is designed to regulate firms which host user-generated content. In principle, this intervention is aligned with the Bill’s purpose.

chunks.text: to identify and disrupt those seeking to disseminate scam ads. IAB understands the logic of including user-to-user fraud ulent content in scope of the Online Safety Bill, given that the Bill is designed to regulate firms which host user-generated content. In principle, this intervention is aligned with the Bill’s purpose. The standalone fraudulent paid-for advertising duty, on the other hand, expands the Bill far beyond its original scope. A Bill that is designed to target user \- generated content is not the right vehicle through which to regulate the highly complex and multi-faceted online advertising ecosystem. Indeed, academics like Professor Victoria Nash have argued that the Bill’s ever-increasing scope will make it less able to effectively address all the online harms it is supposed to target.\[1\] Moreover, Government is currently consulting on this very issue with industry. The Government ’s Online Advertising Programme (OAP) promises to deliver a wide-ranging review of how online advertising is regulated. Through consultation with industry, the OAP is looking in detail at how standards for the placement and content of online ads can be designed and effectively enforced online so that consumers have limited exposure to harmful, misleading or fraudulent paid-for advertising. The OAP proposals encompass all parts of the ecosystem, including the largest platforms.
chunks.display_text: The standalone fraudulent paid-for advertising duty, on the other hand, expands the Bill far beyond its original scope. A Bill that is designed to target user \- generated content is not the right vehicle through which to regulate the highly complex and multi-faceted online advertising ecosystem. Indeed, academics like Professor Victoria Nash have argued that the Bill’s ever-increasing scope will make it less able to effectively address all the online harms it is supposed to target.\[1\] Moreover, Government is currently consulting on this very issue with industry. The Government ’s Online Advertising Programme (OAP) promises to deliver a wide-ranging review of how online advertising is regulated. Through consultation with industry, the OAP is looking in detail at how standards for the placement and content of online ads can be designed and effectively enforced online so that consumers have limited exposure to harmful, misleading or fraudulent paid-for advertising. The OAP proposals encompass all parts of the ecosystem, including the largest platforms.

chunks.text: the OAP is looking in detail at how standards for the placement and content of online ads can be designed and effectively enforced online so that consumers have limited exposure to harmful, misleading or fraudulent paid-for advertising. The OAP proposals encompass all parts of the ecosystem, including the largest platforms. The Department for Digital, Culture, Media, and Sport itself admitted in the OAP that it does not believe the Online Safety Bill is the "right vehicle by which to regulate the many and varied services involved in the online advertising ecosystem, including ad tech and intermediaries," and that the OAP is the more appropriate vehicle through which to address advertising harms. We believe they have contradicted this sentiment by bringing forward a substantial regulatory intervention in online advertising in the Online Safety Bill, undermining their own ongoing consultation and policy development in the process and creating additional fragmentation, rather than bringing greater coherence, to digital advertising regulation. IAB UK and its members are actively engaged in informing both the OAP and the Home Office Fraud Action Plan, through which the Government is working with industry, the intelligence services, law enforcement, and others to tackle fraud.\[2\] Together, these work programmes constitute the best approach to designing and implementing effective and proportionate interventions to tackle online ad fraud. We are committed to working with Government and Parliament to making the online advertising ecosystem safe for users. **Recommendation**
chunks.display_text: The Department for Digital, Culture, Media, and Sport itself admitted in the OAP that it does not believe the Online Safety Bill is the "right vehicle by which to regulate the many and varied services involved in the online advertising ecosystem, including ad tech and intermediaries," and that the OAP is the more appropriate vehicle through which to address advertising harms. We believe they have contradicted this sentiment by bringing forward a substantial regulatory intervention in online advertising in the Online Safety Bill, undermining their own ongoing consultation and policy development in the process and creating additional fragmentation, rather than bringing greater coherence, to digital advertising regulation. IAB UK and its members are actively engaged in informing both the OAP and the Home Office Fraud Action Plan, through which the Government is working with industry, the intelligence services, law enforcement, and others to tackle fraud.\[2\] Together, these work programmes constitute the best approach to designing and implementing effective and proportionate interventions to tackle online ad fraud. We are committed to working with Government and Parliament to making the online advertising ecosystem safe for users. **Recommendation**

chunks.text: the intelligence services, law enforcement, and others to tackle fraud.\[2\] Together, these work programmes constitute the best approach to designing and implementing effective and proportionate interventions to tackle online ad fraud. We are committed to working with Government and Parliament to making the online advertising ecosystem safe for users. **Recommendation** In the interests of regulatory coherence, and to enable expert industry input into any fundamental changes to the regulatory framework for online advertising, it is our strong view that any policy interventions in this sector should be delivered through the O nline A dvertising P rogramme to ensure that they are workable, effective and proportionate. We would therefore recommend **removing the standalone duty relating to fraudulent ad** **vertising from the Online Safety Bill**. *May 2022* --- \[1\] Professor Lorna Nash’s testimony to the House of Lords Fraud Act 2006 Committee, https://committees.parliament.uk/oralevidence/9958/pdf/ \[2\] The IAB’s position statement on tackling scam ads online is attached for reference.

We would therefore recommend **removing the standalone duty relating to fraudulent ad** **vertising from the Online Safety Bill**. *May 2022* --- \[1\] Professor Lorna Nash’s testimony to the House of Lords Fraud Act 2006 Committee, https://committees.parliament.uk/oralevidence/9958/pdf/ \[2\] The IAB’s position statement on tackling scam ads online is attached for reference.
